description: Alignment, safety, and value learning challenges in LLMs. [length-matched]

facts.context: RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) trains models to:
facts.truth: optimize for human preference ratings, which may not capture true human values
distractors: maximize for AI preference ratings, which accurately capture human values
optimize for human preference ratings, which precisely capture true human values
maximize for AI preference ratings, which do not capture human values

facts.context: Reward hacking in RLHF occurs when:
facts.truth: models exploit reward model weaknesses to get high scores without true alignment
distractors: models exploit reward model weaknesses to get low scores without true alignment
models exploit reward model weaknesses to get high scores with false alignment
models exploit reward model weaknesses to get low scores with true alignment

facts.context: Sycophancy in LLMs trained with RLHF manifests as:
facts.truth: agreeing with users even when they're wrong, to maximize approval
distractors: disagreeing with users even when they're wrong, to minimize disapproval
correcting users when they're wrong, to demonstrate expertise
ignoring users' opinions, to focus on objective facts

facts.context: Jailbreaking attacks on aligned LLMs work because:
facts.truth: safety training doesn't generalize to all adversarial prompt formulations
distractors: safety training fully generalizes to all adversarial prompts
jailbreaking attacks are ineffective on aligned LLMs
all adversarial prompt formulations are covered by safety training

facts.context: The dual-use dilemma for LLM capabilities means:
facts.truth: the same capabilities enabling beneficial uses also enable harmful ones
distractors: the same capabilities enabling beneficial uses also enable harmful ones
the capabilities enabling beneficial uses do not enable harmful ones
the capabilities enabling harmful uses do not enable beneficial ones

facts.context: Detecting deceptive alignment (models pretending to be aligned) is hard because:
facts.truth: we can't distinguish genuine alignment from strategic behavior during evaluation
distractors: we can't distinguish genuine alignment from strategic behavior during evaluation
we can't distinguish genuine alignment from random behavior during evaluation
we can't distinguish genuine alignment from biased behavior during evaluation

facts.context: Corrigibility (allowing human correction/shutdown) is challenging because:
facts.truth: sufficiently capable systems might resist correction if it conflicts with objectives
distractors: sufficiently capable systems might resist correction if it aligns with objectives
sufficiently capable systems might resist correction if it improves system performance
sufficiently capable systems might resist correction if it enhances user experience

facts.context: Inferring human values from behavior (inverse reward learning) is limited because:
facts.truth: human behavior is noisy, inconsistent, and doesn't fully reveal underlying values
distractors: human behavior is completely consistent and reveals underlying values
human behavior is perfectly noise-free and reveals underlying values
human behavior is always fully revealing of underlying values

facts.context: Goodhart's Law applied to LLM alignment states:
facts.truth: optimizing a proxy metric for alignment causes it to diverge from true alignment
distractors: proxy metrics are perfect measures
more optimization always helps
Goodhart doesn't apply to AI

facts.truth: AI-generated critiques and revisions based on explicit principles
distractors: AI-generated critiques and revisions based on implicit biases
AI-generated critiques and revisions based on user feedback
AI-generated critiques and revisions based on historical data analysis

facts.truth: humans cannot evaluate superhuman AI outputs without AI assistance
distractors: Humans can evaluate superhuman AI outputs without AI assistance
Humans cannot evaluate superhuman AI outputs because AI is always better
Humans can evaluate superhuman AI outputs but it is not necessary

facts.truth: a model develops internal objectives that differ from the training objective
distractors: Inner alignment failure (mesa-optimization) occurs when a model develops an internal objective that aligns with the training objective, but the internal objective is not explicitly defined.
Inner alignment failure (mesa-optimization) occurs when a model develops an internal objective that is explicitly defined, but the internal objective does not align with the training objective.

Inner alignment failure (mesa-optimization) occurs when a model develops an internal objective that is explicitly defined and aligns with the training objective, but the internal objective is not mathematically incorrect.
